Count GL8(F2) elements with (x+1)^2 and irreducible-cubic primary data

Let V be an 8-dimensional vector space over 𝔽2, and choose T uniformly at random from GL(V).

Let q(x)=x^3+x+1 \in 𝔽2[x]. (Note that q has no root in 𝔽2, hence is irreducible.)

Define the event X that T satisfies all of the following:
1) χ_T(x) = (x+1)^2 q(x)^2,
2) μ_T(x) = (x+1)^2 q(x),
3) dim ker(T-I)=1.

Let M = |GL(V)|  \cdot  P(X). (Equivalently, M is the number of operators T\inGL(V) satisfying X.)

Compute M mod 1000, as the unique integer in {0,1,...,999} congruent to M modulo 1000.

Solution.
Step 1 (reinterpret_M_as_count)
Since T is uniform on GL(V),
  P(X) = (# of T \in GL(V) satisfying X) / |GL(V)|.
Thus
  M = |GL(V)| \cdot P(X) = # { T \in GL(V) : T satisfies X }.

So we only need to count such T.

Step 2 (deduce_primary_structure)
Because (x+1) and q(x) are coprime in 𝔽2[x], the primary decomposition theorem gives a T-stable direct sum
  V = V_{1} ⊕ V_{q},
where:
- V_{1} is the (x+1)-primary component, and
- V_{q} is the q-primary component.

From χ_T(x)=(x+1)^2 q(x)^2 we get:
- dim(V_{1}) = 2,
- dim(V_{q}) = 2 \cdot deg(q) = 2 \cdot 3 = 6.

Moreover, μ_T(x)=(x+1)^2 q(x) means:
- on V_{1}, the minimal polynomial is (x+1)^2,
- on V_{q}, the minimal polynomial is q.

Step 3 (determine_unipotent_Jordan_type)
On V_{1} (a 2-dimensional space), μ_{T|V1}=(x+1)^2 forces the Jordan form at eigenvalue 1 to have a block of size 2. Since the total size is 2, the only possibility is the single block partition (2). In particular,
  dim ker(T-I) on V_{1} equals the number of Jordan blocks = 1,
which matches the given kernel constraint, so the (x+1)-primary part is uniquely determined up to similarity.

Let C1 denote the centralizer of this 2×2 unipotent Jordan block inside GL_2(𝔽2).
A commuting matrix must be of the form aI+bN where N is the nilpotent off-diagonal matrix (N^2=0). Over 𝔽2, invertibility forces a=1, while b is free in 𝔽2. Hence
  |C1| = 2.

Step 4 (q-primary component as an extension-field module)
On V_{q}, we have χ_{T|Vq}=q^2 and μ_{T|Vq}=q. Since q is irreducible and the minimal polynomial is squarefree, T|_{Vq} is semisimple with rational canonical form having invariant factors q and q.

Equivalently, V_{q} is a 2-dimensional vector space over 𝔽_{2^3}=𝔽8, and T acts as multiplication by a fixed element α\in𝔽8 with minimal polynomial q over 𝔽2. Any 𝔽2-linear map commuting with "multiply by α" is exactly an 𝔽8-linear map, so the centralizer on V_q is
  Cq ≅ GL_2(𝔽8).

Therefore
  |Cq| = |GL_2(8)| = (8^2-1)(8^2-8) = 63 \cdot 56 = 3528.

Step 5 (compute_centralizer_sizes and uniqueness of the similarity class)
Because the two primary parts correspond to coprime polynomials, any matrix commuting with T must preserve the decomposition V=V1⊕Vq, and the centralizer factors as a direct product:
  C(T) ≅ C1 × Cq,
so
  |C(T)| = |C1| \cdot |Cq| = 2 \cdot 3528 = 7056.

The data (χ_T, μ_T, dim ker(T-I)) uniquely determine the similarity class:
- V splits uniquely into dimensions 2 and 6 primary parts,
- the (x+1)-part is forced to be Jordan type (2),
- the q-part is forced to have invariant factors (q,q).
Hence the number of T satisfying X equals the size of one conjugacy class:
  M = |GL_8(𝔽2)| / |C(T)|.

Step 6 (reduce_mod_1000_without_full_group_order)
Use the standard formula
  |GL_n(𝔽2)| = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} (2^n - 2^i).
For n=8:
  |GL_8(𝔽2)| = (256-1)(256-2)(256-4)(256-8)(256-16)(256-32)(256-64)(256-128)
             = 255 \cdot 254 \cdot 252 \cdot 248 \cdot 240 \cdot 224 \cdot 192 \cdot 128.

Also factor the centralizer size:
  7056 = 2^4 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 7^2 = 16 \cdot 9 \cdot 49.

So
  M = (255 \cdot 254 \cdot 252 \cdot 248 \cdot 240 \cdot 224 \cdot 192 \cdot 128) / (16 \cdot 9 \cdot 49).

Cancel step-by-step:
- Cancel 16 with 128: 128/16 = 8.
- Cancel 9 with 252: 252/9 = 28.
- Cancel 49 = 7 \cdot 7 using 28 and 224:
  28/7 = 4 and 224/7 = 32.

After cancellations:
  M = 255  \cdot  254  \cdot  4  \cdot  248  \cdot  240  \cdot  32  \cdot  192  \cdot  8.

Now compute modulo 1000 by modular multiplication:
- 255 \cdot 254 = 64770 \equiv 770 (mod 1000)
- 770 \cdot 4 = 3080 \equiv 80
- 80 \cdot 248 = 19840 \equiv 840
- 840 \cdot 240 = 201600 \equiv 600
- 600 \cdot 32 = 19200 \equiv 200
- 200 \cdot 192 = 38400 \equiv 400
- 400 \cdot 8 = 3200 \equiv 200  (mod 1000)

Therefore,
  M \equiv 200 (mod 1000).

Answer: 200.

Uniqueness Test
1) (Primary decomposition is forced.)
Since (x+1) and q are coprime and χ_T=(x+1)^2 q^2, V decomposes uniquely as V=V1⊕Vq with dim V1=2 and dim Vq=6, and both summands are T-invariant.

2) (The (x+1)-primary structure is forced.)
On V1, μ=(x+1)^2 forces a Jordan block of size 2; because dim V1=2, the only possible partition is (2). Hence dim ker(T-I)=1 is automatic and consistent, and there is exactly one similarity type on V1.

3) (The q-primary structure is forced.)
On Vq, χ=q^2 and μ=q imply the invariant factors are q and q (no higher powers), i.e. the q-primary part is semisimple with exactly two identical irreducible blocks. Thus there is exactly one similarity type on Vq.

4) (Single conjugacy class and unique count.)
Because the similarity type is uniquely determined on each coprime primary component, T is uniquely determined up to similarity in GL_8(𝔽2). Therefore the number of such T is uniquely the conjugacy class size |GL_8(𝔽2)|/|C(T)|, and reducing modulo 1000 gives a unique residue 200.

200
